Scouting Summary

Common themes

  • Left Orthodox, averaging 79.6 kph (up to 85.8).
  • Typical length is 4-5m (their good length); median 4.4 m.
  • Stock ball is full in the channel, turning away, drifting in, hitting the top of off (25% of deliveries).
  • Goes round the wicket 97% of the time against right-handers.
  • Turns it 3.6° on average; 19% of balls turn ≥5°.
  • Across an over he repeats his length ball after ball.

Biggest threats

  • Most dangerous length is Full (9 wkts, 3.1% adjusted strike).
  • Danger pitching line: Stumps.
  • Takes 27% of wickets pitching full in the 4th-stump channel.
  • Beats the bat 6.4% of tracked balls (false-shot 19.5%).
  • Gets you LBW more than most — 33% of his wickets (1.7× the typical rate for this type).
  • 40% of catches are behind the wicket (keeper / slips / gully); most often to slip: 1st, cover: short extra, long off: regulation.
  • Very repeatable with his length — using your feet to change his length is more effective than waiting for a loose ball.

Areas to exploit

  • Concedes most runs pitching full on the stumps (18% of runs).
  • Away from full, the wicket threat drops off — look to score in the less-productive lengths.
  • Most of his runs leak through the leg side (51%) — his main scoring release.
  • Cash in when he goes good length outside off (economy 3.4, beats the bat only 18%).

Solid line = career, dotted = last 3 years (where enough recent balls). Percentile within same-type peers (grey = the peer distribution, line = this bowler). Release/crease vs hand × pace/spin; movement/speed/repeatability vs pace/spin. Release & speed are modern-era (2017+ / partial). Crease variation = how much he moves his release point sideways across the crease from ball to ball (within his main angle): a high percentile = he varies where he lets the ball go a lot, a low percentile = he releases from the same spot every ball. Repeatability = length consistency over his stock-length band (2–11 m, so deliberate yorkers and bouncers don't count as poor control): a high percentile = tighter, more metronomic lengths than peers; a low percentile = he varies his length more. A marker at the very edge = he sits beyond the typical peer range on that trait.

Stock Ball & Ball Types (vs right-handers) ▶ watch stock balls

Stock ball — full in the channel, turning away, drifting in, hitting the top of off (25% of deliveries, economy 1.13). Minor variations: full on the stumps (22%) and full outside off (21%).
Ball type (pitch length × pitching line)Movement%EconWktsBeaten %
full in the channel ▶turning away, drifting in25%1.13318%
full on the stumps ▶turning away, drifting in22%1.98324%
full outside off ▶turning away, drifting in21%3.18319%
a good length in the channel ▶turning away, drifting in11%3.26013%
a good length outside off ▶turning away, drifting in8%3.35118%
a good length on the stumps ▶turning away, drifting in5%3.00127%
Ball type = pitch-length band × pitching-line region. Movement = swing in the air + seam/turn off the pitch, whichever is material, dominant first (ball-tracking, tracked balls only; blank if too few). Beaten % = false-shot rate on tracked balls. The stock-ball line above also notes where it passes the stumps.

Scoring Profile (vs right-handers)

53% of his runs come in boundaries — a boundary every 19 balls; most runs go to the leg side (51%); the sweep hurts most (8 fours/sixes at 3.4 runs/ball).

Pitch Maps & Scoring

At the Stumps (wickets)
Wicket balls pass the stumps mostly at wide of 6th — pitches around stumps and moves it back.
Where They're Scored Off
Runs come mostly on the leg side (61%).
Where They Pitch It
Pitches it full in the 4th-stump channel most often (23%); rarely drops short (0%).
Where Wickets Come From
Wickets come mostly from balls pitched full in the 4th-stump channel, but strikes most often pitching on the stumps.

Speed & Spells

He holds his pace across spells, keeps his pace into the 2nd innings, and is as quick on the final day as day one.

Speed by Day

Sequencing & Over Construction

Relentlessly consistent — repeats his length ball after ball (length spread ±1.0 m; more repeatable than 82% of spin bowlers); holds a steady length right through the over; and holds the same crease position through the over.

Length spread = std-dev of pitch length (smaller = more repeatable). The table shows how his length, scoring and wicket rate shift across the six balls of an over. Set-up lines compare each ball with the previous delivery in the same over (career, all batters).

Release Point & Crease Use

A low, skiddy release point (lower than 88% of left-arm spin); releases wide of the stumps (106 cm out, wider than 93% of left-arm spin).

A moderate spinner of the ball; extracts steep bounce; gets sharp drift in the air.
Generates about average turn and well above avg drift for a spin bowler; turns it away more to right-hand batters (100%).

Beats the bat most at Full / Stumps (33% of play-and-misses). Overall he beats the bat 6.4% of tracked balls (false-shot 19.5%, n=437).
Test-match career data. Beaten/false-shot use ~38% shot-quality tracking; turn/drift ~30% ball-tracking. Catches split by fielding position (DeliveryFielders view); some catches have no recorded position. Danger zones use empirical-Bayes shrinkage (≥3 wkts to qualify).
